basalt-run-design-system-2-alert.name: Alert
basalt-run-design-system-2-alert.description: Alert communicates important information that demands immediate attention without interrupting the user's task.

basalt.usage: Reach for Alert when system state changes require acknowledgment—validation errors during form submission, confirmation of destructive actions, or time-sensitive notifications. Place alerts contextually near the affected content rather than at page top to reduce cognitive load. Avoid stacking multiple alerts; prioritize the most critical message.
basalt.never: Don't use Alert for success confirmations that don't require action — success states belong in Toast instead. Toast disappears automatically and doesn't block layout, preserving focus for task continuation.
basalt.a11y: Alert must have role="alert" to announce dynamically inserted content to screen readers. Place the alert in the DOM before injecting its message to ensure NVDA and JAWS announce updates (WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages).

basalt-run-design-system-2-badge.name: Badge
basalt-run-design-system-2-badge.description: Badge displays concise, labeled metadata or status indicators in a compact visual form.

basalt.usage: Use badges to denote status, categories, or counts where brevity is essential—such as showing workflow states (pending, approved, rejected) or flagging new feature availability. Badges work well in lists, tables, and headers where space is constrained and quick visual scanning matters.
basalt.never: Don't use badges for interactive actions — badges are static indicators, not buttons. Use Button or ButtonGroup instead when user interaction is required.
basalt.a11y: Badge must have role="status" or role="img" with aria-label to convey meaning to screen readers, ensuring non-sighted users understand the badge's semantic purpose (WCAG 1.1.1).

basalt-run-design-system-2-button.name: Button
basalt-run-design-system-2-button.description: Button initiates an action or navigation when activated.

basalt.usage: Use Button for primary actions within a page or modal, such as form submission or confirming a destructive operation. Buttons should represent the most important action available to the user in any given context. Reserve this component for interactions that trigger immediate results rather than navigation to new routes—use Link for cross-page navigation.
basalt.never: Don't nest interactive elements like dropdowns or popovers inside a Button — screen readers will announce conflicting roles and keyboard navigation breaks. Use a ButtonGroup or composed component pattern instead.
basalt.a11y: Button must have role="button" and keyboard support for Space and Enter keys (WCAG 2.1 Level A, 2.1.1 Keyboard). Ensure visible focus indicator meets 3:1 contrast ratio minimum.

basalt-run-design-system-2-card.name: Card
basalt-run-design-system-2-card.description: Card containers group related content with a subtle border and background treatment.

basalt.usage: Reach for Card when organizing information into discrete, scannable sections—like a pricing tier, settings panel, or dashboard metric. Use it to create visual hierarchy without heavy visual weight, keeping the interface calm and focused.
basalt.never: Don't nest Cards within Cards to create visual depth — this fragments information architecture and confuses content relationships. Use a single Card with internal sections or typography hierarchy instead.
basalt.a11y: Card must not define semantic structure via visual treatment alone; reserve Cards for layout grouping only. Content inside requires proper heading hierarchy (h1–h6) and landmark roles (main, section, article) to establish document outline.

basalt-run-design-system-2-checkbox.name: Checkbox
basalt-run-design-system-2-checkbox.description: Checkbox permits users to select one or more options from a set of mutually independent choices.

basalt.usage: Reach for checkboxes when users need to select multiple items from a list, such as filtering search results or opting into notification preferences. Use a single checkbox when confirming agreement to terms or enabling a feature. Avoid checkboxes for mutually exclusive options; use radio buttons instead.
basalt.never: Don't nest checkboxes within other checkboxes — this breaks predictable interaction patterns and confuses focus management. Use a checkbox group or collapsible section instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="checkbox" with aria-checked reflecting true, false, or mixed states. Support Space and Enter keys for toggling without requiring mouse interaction.

basalt-run-design-system-2-input.name: Input
basalt-run-design-system-2-input.description: Input captures single-line text entry from users.

basalt.usage: Use Input for collecting focused data like emails, usernames, or search queries. Pair with clear labels and validation feedback to guide users toward correct entries. In a sign-up flow, an Input for email precedes submission only when the format validates.
basalt.never: Don't rely on placeholder text as a label — placeholders vanish on input focus, breaking WCAG 2.1 SC 3.3.2 compliance. Use a persistent label element instead.
basalt.a11y: Bind a <label> element to Input via the for attribute matching the input's id; screen readers require this association to announce the field's purpose (WCAG 2.1 SC 1.3.1).

basalt-run-design-system-2-modal.name: Modal
basalt-run-design-system-2-modal.description: Modal presents focused content that requires user interaction before returning to the underlying page.

basalt.usage: Use modals for confirmations, forms, or critical information that demands immediate attention. Reserve modals for genuinely blocking workflows—when a user attempts to delete their account or authorize a payment, for example. Overuse creates friction; prefer inline solutions or drawers for secondary tasks.
basalt.never: Don't trigger modals on page load or as a first-time experience — users perceive unexpected overlays as intrusive. Use Dialog instead, which surfaces intentionally without blocking the page.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="dialog" with aria-modal="true" and trap focus within the modal until dismissal. Ensure the close button or escape key returns focus to the trigger element (WCAG 2.4.3).

basalt-run-design-system-2-select.name: Select
basalt-run-design-system-2-select.description: Select presents a list of mutually exclusive options in a compact, closed interface that expands on interaction.

basalt.usage: Use Select when users must choose one option from a predefined set and space is constrained—such as filtering a table, selecting a timezone, or picking a team context. Reserve this component for lists exceeding 3-4 items; use Radio or SegmentedControl for smaller sets. Select reduces cognitive load by hiding options until needed.
basalt.never: Don't populate Select with dynamic data that changes during the user's session without explicit interaction—this breaks predictability. Use a Combobox instead if users need to search, filter, or input values not in the preset list.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="listbox" with role="option" children and ensure keyboard navigation via arrow keys with Home/End support. WCAG 2.1 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) requires the trigger button expose the currently selected value to screen readers via aria-label or aria-labelledby.

basalt-run-design-system-2-toggle.name: Toggle
basalt-run-design-system-2-toggle.description: Toggle switches a single boolean state on or off.

basalt.usage: Use toggle for binary settings where the state change takes effect immediately, such as enabling notifications, activating dark mode, or toggling feature flags in account settings. Reach for toggle when the action requires no confirmation and the on/off meaning is self-evident from context or label.
basalt.never: Don't use toggle for choices between multiple options — it only represents two states. Use Segmented Control or Select instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="switch" with aria-checked reflecting current state. Support Space and Enter keys to toggle, and ensure focus is visible with a 2px outline.

basalt-run-design-system-2-tooltip.name: Tooltip
basalt-run-design-system-2-tooltip.description: Tooltip displays contextual information on hover or focus, appearing near a trigger element.

basalt.usage: Use Tooltip to explain abbreviated labels, icon-only buttons, or unfamiliar terminology without cluttering the interface. Place tooltips on form fields to clarify input requirements, or on icon buttons in dense toolbars where space is constrained. Avoid tooltips for essential information—they disappear on interaction and are invisible to mobile users.
basalt.never: Don't place interactive content inside a tooltip — keyboard users cannot reach it. Use a Popover or Dialog instead.
basalt.a11y: Set role="tooltip" on the tooltip element and aria-describedby on the trigger to create the association. Tooltip content must remain visible for 5 seconds minimum (WCAG 2.5.1) and be dismissible via Escape key.
